title: Why Self-Management is the Foundation of Great Leadership with Lynda Harvey
guestBio: Lynda Harvey is a former corporate leader at Verizon and Nordstrom who has transformed into a viral TikTok educator and entrepreneur. She now runs a thriving micro-learning platform focused on social leadership while raising two teenagers and helping other leaders develop self-awareness and emotional intelligence.
excerpt: Former corporate executive Lynda Harvey reveals why the best leaders master themselves first. Discover how to build self-awareness, lead with calm confidence, and transform your invisible work into recognized leadership skills.
metaDescription: Learn why self-management comes before people management with former Verizon exec Lynda Harvey. Essential leadership advice for working moms.
keyTakeaways: Self-management is the foundational skill that must be mastered before you can effectively manage others.
Mothers naturally develop many management skills through daily parenting that translate directly to workplace leadership.
Knowing who you don't want to be as a leader is just as important as defining who you want to become.
Tactical missions and small daily practices build leadership confidence more effectively than waiting for formal titles.
Emotional intelligence and practical empathy are becoming essential leadership skills in the modern workplace.
Building a personal brand around your expertise can create new career opportunities beyond traditional corporate paths.
Self-awareness about your triggers and reactions allows you to lead from a place of calm rather than chaos.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership doesn't start in the boardroom—it starts in your own living room.
You have to know who you don't want to be just as much as you know who you are.

timestamps.label: Intro: Reframing Invisible Work as Leadership
Lynda's Corporate Journey: Verizon → Nordstrom → Entrepreneur
Knowing Who You Don't Want to Be

content:
What if everything you've been told about leadership has been backwards? What if the path to becoming an exceptional leader doesn't start with managing others, but with mastering yourself first? This transformative conversation between Aimee Rickabus and leadership expert Lynda Harvey will completely shift how you think about your own leadership potential.

Lynda Harvey knows something about reinvention. After building a successful corporate career at powerhouse companies like Verizon and Nordstrom, she made the bold decision to step away from the traditional corporate ladder and build something entirely her own. Today, she's a viral TikTok educator and entrepreneur running a thriving micro-learning platform that's helping thousands of people develop their leadership skills. But her most important credential might be the one that's often overlooked: she's a mother of two teenagers who has learned to lead from the ground up.

The Hidden Leadership Training Ground

Here's something that might surprise you: some of the most effective leadership training doesn't happen in corporate conference rooms or executive retreats. It happens at 6 AM when you're coordinating breakfast, lunch prep, and permission slips while also preparing for your own workday. Lynda's perspective on why mothers make exceptional managers isn't just encouraging—it's revolutionary.

The daily juggling act of motherhood requires you to develop skills that MBA programs spend months trying to teach. You learn to prioritize ruthlessly when everything feels urgent. You master the art of clear communication when you have thirty seconds to give instructions before someone melts down. You develop an intuitive understanding of what motivates different personality types because each of your children responds to completely different approaches.

But here's what Lynda emphasizes: these aren't just survival skills. These are leadership competencies that translate directly into workplace success. The challenge isn't that working mothers lack leadership abilities—it's that we've been trained not to recognize our own expertise.

Self-Management: The Foundation Nobody Talks About

The conversation takes a crucial turn when Lynda addresses what she calls the most overlooked aspect of leadership development: self-management. Before you can effectively guide others, you need to understand your own triggers, patterns, and reactions. This isn't about achieving some impossible standard of perfection—it's about developing enough self-awareness that you can lead from intention rather than reaction.

Self-management looks like recognizing when you're about to respond from a place of stress and choosing to pause instead. It means understanding which situations drain your energy and which ones fuel you, then structuring your days accordingly. It involves knowing your communication style and adapting it to what others need to hear, rather than just what feels natural to say.

This level of self-awareness doesn't develop overnight, and Lynda is refreshingly honest about her own journey. The path from corporate executive to entrepreneur while raising teenagers hasn't been a smooth ascent. It's required her to constantly examine her own assumptions about success, leadership, and what it means to create a life that works for her family.

The Power of Tactical Missions

One of the most practical concepts Lynda introduces is the idea of tactical missions—small, specific ways to practice leadership skills without waiting for someone to give you permission or a formal title. These aren't grand gestures or dramatic career pivots. They're intentional practices that build your leadership muscle over time.

A tactical mission might look like taking the lead on a challenging conversation you've been avoiding. It could mean volunteering to facilitate a team meeting or offering to mentor a newer colleague. The key is that these missions are chosen deliberately, not randomly, and they're aligned with the kind of leader you want to become.

This approach is particularly powerful for women who have been waiting for someone else to recognize their leadership potential. Instead of waiting for permission, you start practicing. Instead of hoping someone will notice your capabilities, you begin demonstrating them in small, consistent ways that build both your confidence and your reputation.

Knowing Who You Don't Want to Be

Perhaps the most profound insight from this conversation is Lynda's emphasis on defining yourself through negative space—understanding who you don't want to be as clearly as you understand who you do want to become. This isn't about judgment or criticism of other leadership styles. It's about clarity and authenticity.

Maybe you don't want to be the leader who manages through fear or intimidation. Perhaps you've experienced micromanagement and know that's not the approach you want to take with your own team. You might have witnessed leaders who prioritized results over relationships and decided that's not aligned with your values.

This kind of reverse engineering of your leadership identity is incredibly powerful because it helps you make decisions in real time. When you're clear about the kind of leader you don't want to be, it becomes much easier to choose how to respond in challenging situations.

The Entrepreneurial Pivot

Lynda's transition from corporate leadership to building her own platform offers a masterclass in leveraging your expertise in new ways. Her success on TikTok and subsequent business development didn't happen by accident—it was the result of consistently sharing valuable insights in an accessible format and building genuine relationships with her audience.

What's particularly inspiring about her journey is how she's maintained her focus on practical, actionable advice rather than theoretical concepts. Her micro-learning approach recognizes that busy professionals need leadership development that fits into their real lives, not abstract theories that require hours of implementation.

Leadership for the Real World

This conversation ultimately reframes leadership as something that's both more accessible and more demanding than traditional definitions suggest. It's more accessible because you don't need to wait for a title or formal authority to begin developing and practicing leadership skills. It's more demanding because it requires the ongoing work of self-examination, growth, and intentional choice-making.

The leadership skills you're already developing—whether in your current role, in your family, or in your community—are real and valuable. The question isn't whether you have what it takes to be a leader. The question is whether you're ready to recognize and develop what you already have while committing to the ongoing work of managing yourself with the same intentionality you'd bring to managing others.

Your leadership journey doesn't start when someone gives you permission. It starts when you decide to see yourself clearly, choose your responses consciously, and commit to showing up as the kind of leader you want to be—one small tactical mission at a time.

quiz.title: What's Your Leadership Starting Point?
quiz.description: Discover where you're beginning your leadership journey and how to build on the management skills you already have. Based on insights from episode 30 with leadership expert Lynda Harvey.

questions.question: When you see something at work or in your community that could be running more smoothly, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Jump in and start organizing people to fix it
Watch and analyze the situation to understand all the moving pieces first
Quietly start doing what you can to improve it yourself
Feel frustrated but hesitate to speak up because it's 'not your job'

questions.question: When someone asks you about your management or leadership experience, you typically:

options.text: Share examples easily—you've been leading in various contexts for a while
Focus on times you've supported others or helped behind the scenes
Mention projects you've completed but downplay the leadership aspects
Struggle to come up with examples because you don't see yourself as a 'leader type'

questions.question: Your biggest concern about taking on more leadership responsibility is:

options.text: Making sure you don't lose touch with what made you effective in the first place
Whether you'll be able to make tough decisions when emotions are running high
Having to become more vocal and visible than feels natural to you
Becoming the type of manager or leader that you've complained about in the past

questions.question: When you think about the leadership skills you already have, you:

options.text: Can clearly identify several areas where you're already strong
Recognize you're good at reading people and understanding group dynamics
Know you're dependable and good at follow-through, but wonder if that's enough
Have trouble seeing your everyday problem-solving as 'real' leadership skills

questions.question: If you had to choose the most important quality in a leader, you'd pick:

options.text: The ability to inspire and mobilize people toward a common goal
Emotional intelligence and the skill to manage different personality types
Consistency and the ability to create stable, trustworthy environments
Self-awareness and the commitment to not repeat the mistakes of bad leaders

questions.question: Your relationship with the idea of 'self-management' is:

options.text: You're pretty good at it and see how it connects to managing others effectively
You're very aware of your own patterns and triggers, maybe sometimes too aware
You manage yourself well day-to-day but don't always advocate for your own needs
You're still figuring out how to manage your own reactions before trying to lead others

results.title: The Natural Leader
results.description: You already demonstrate strong leadership qualities, often stepping up without being asked and naturally organizing those around you. Your challenge isn't learning to lead—it's recognizing that you're already doing it and giving yourself permission to own that identity. You might be underestimating the management skills you've developed through life experience. This episode will help you see how to leverage what you already know and take your leadership to the next level.

results.title: The Thoughtful Observer
results.description: You prefer to understand all the dynamics before taking action, and you've developed keen insights about what makes effective leadership by watching others. Your superpower is emotional intelligence and your ability to read situations deeply. You might doubt whether you're 'leadership material,' but your thoughtful approach is exactly what modern workplaces need. This episode will show you how self-awareness and practical empathy are becoming the most essential leadership skills.

results.title: The Quiet Achiever
results.description: You get things done consistently and reliably, often preferring to lead by example rather than taking center stage. Your strength lies in building trust through your actions and creating stability for others. You might think you need to be more outgoing or charismatic to be a leader, but your steady approach is incredibly valuable. This episode will help you understand how tactical missions and small daily practices can build your leadership confidence without requiring a complete personality overhaul.

results.title: The Reluctant Leader
results.description: You have strong opinions about how things should be done and can clearly see what's not working, but you're hesitant to step into formal leadership roles. You might know exactly who you don't want to be as a leader, but you're still figuring out who you do want to become. Your critical thinking skills are actually a leadership asset waiting to be channeled. This episode will resonate with you because it explores how knowing who you don't want to be is just as important as defining your leadership identity.

transcript: Let's [Music] have some fun. >> Hi everyone, welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host, Amy Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur, and mama of six. Today's guest is someone who knows management inside and out, not just from climbing the corporate ladder, but from teaching others to lead in their own way. Linda Harvey has worked for major corporations like Verizon and Nordstrom's. And now she's on a mission to train new managers, especially those who never got the playbook. When companies didn't want to spend on training, Linda took her message to Tik Tok, building a microlearning platform on social leadership that's grown into a self-paced school and thriving community. As a mom of two teenagers, she knows that the skills we use to run our households can translate directly into leadership success. Today we're talking about self-management, people management, and how to lead with calm, empathy, and confidence. Plus, why knowing who you don't want to be might be just as important as knowing who you are. >> All right, Linda Harvey is in the house. Hi. >> Hi. So good to have you. >> I'm so excited to be here. >> I am thrilled. I was so excited when you reached out to us on social. You have such a big following. Totally bow down to you on that. It's amazing what you've been been able to build. >> Oh, well, thank you. You know, it's been a bit of a whirlwind for sure, but um really kind of an amazing ride. >> That is fantastic. Yeah. Do tell me. Okay, we could get into your origin story. So, you've led teams in major corporations like Verizon, Nordstrom, and now you teach entry level managers. Let's go back. Let's go back to how you got into all this in the first place. >> Yeah. So, we'll go back really far and we'll say like, you know, I think that I was always described as a very bossy little girl. Uh, which I now call executive leadership skills. So, I think, you know, and and you know, forever it's been kind of a commanding like, oh, you command a room, you do the things. Uh, so it's something that I fell kind of into management, right? I just kind of fell into management. I think as so many people do and as and as I've gone on this journey I fall I find that that's the case for most people. So, I just kind of fell into management and I continued to just kind of go and I'm very ambitious and so I just kind of kept kind of climbing the ladder and when CO hit, I was like, "Hey, let's throw one more log on this fire and let's go get my masters." Like, let's let's just go get my masters amongst all of the things that are going on. So I went back and got my masters and you know there I I toiled over do I get my MBA and I was like I really don't want an MBA. I really just wanted to focus on management and leadership and and I think an MBA does that. No no no slight to anybody who's done an MBA but I really wanted to double down on leadership and management because I loved it so much. I just had found that through the years I really enjoyed helping people develop and I really helped I really enjoyed like helping people succeed in their career and I thought well let's just get my my masters in that. So I doubled down and I did that. And as I was going through this experience, I was learning a lot, right? Like and I had been in management at this point in time for like 20 years. And I was just now learning that there was like documented styles of like actual like studied academically studied man. I just thought that was all made up. I just thought like people wrote books for like whatever. Uh, and I found all of these things and that really opened my eyes to, oh, we need to like be more intentional with how we're managing. We need to we need to understand number one, what are the styles that we're leaning towards? What are the goods and bads of those, right? But then also like, how can we be more intentional? And I really started to lean into this idea and I was managing people and I I landed someplace in my career that I was I felt so secure in with my skills but I noticed that my senior leadership the people above me really lacked the skills to manage and and I I'm talking like fundamental skills and the guidance that I was being given and I was like absolutely not. this is this is not going to work. We can't keep doing this. We've got to be better. And so I decided to just like take the leap and start training people and start like my own company. So, I started my own company where I could partner with companies to help teach their managers, help train their new managers because I felt like that was where the biggest opportunity was because managers step into their role and they're just like deer in the headlights. They have no idea what's going on and so they just like duck and hide and hope for the best and they mimic what they've seen whether it's good or bad. And I thought like I could make the biggest difference and the biggest impact with it people who were newer to management where we can like remold and like develop them. So I started doing that and then I decided well let's add public speaking to the gig too like let's just do that. So I started I I speak in conferences and symposiums and all the things and then I still felt like my reach wasn't large enough and that's when I took to social media and I created like my own management community that people can join and learn the basic kind of tactical fundamental skills while still trying to figure out who they are. So and that's how you and I connected. >> Yeah, that is amazing. And so you like basically and you went out out to Tik Tok. So a lot of corporate corporate leaders wouldn't think of Tik Tok as a training tool, but you've built a real following there and like a big one. Yeah. >> Uh how did you go from quick social media tips to running a full learning platform like on school with your community? >> Um so you know I think that they were kind of symbiotic really. it was more of a hey I'm teaching these things and people would reach out and say well do you coach or do you do these things >> and so I just kind of sprung into building you know unfortunately um the world the the digital world is so amazing right it's so amazing and the school platform hosts people just like me host communities just like mine where you can go on and you can have your classroom you can have all of your stuff put in there but then you can connect with people and you have a community that you can lock down and you don't have to put it on other social media platforms where people are just trolling your clients to have them be their clients. So, this was really great. It gave me a place where I could turn it on really quickly instead of having to spend years or um so much time and energy and money in developing my own app. I was able to just like quickly springboard into this platform and then spin up kind of my own space and my own app. >> That is so cool. I mean, I'm so glad that you turned me on to that. It would be such a great place for us to have the manage her community too at some point when we get there. You know, I thought, wow, this is so neat. I'm going to learn so much from you. And it's like I always say, moms make great managers. And you've said it too, moms make great managers. Can you share some of the ways that parenting has shaped your leadership philosophy? >> That's such a great question. Uh, yeah. So, I have said forever that I'm a better mom because I'm a manager and I'm a better manager because I'm a mom. And really, I think that there's there's a lot of like parallels, right, when it comes to being a mom and a manager. Because when you become a mom, you like you're like, "Okay, this is the thing I'm going to do." And then you're a mom and they hand you a human and they're like, "Have fun." And I feel like it's the exact same thing with managers. You're like, "I guess this is the thing that I do. This is the next growth step for me." And then they're like, "Great. Here's humans. Bye." And it really is so much of like this sink or swim scenario where you're just like, "Okay, I'm going to go out there and I'm going to do the things." But the thing the the things that I've learned fundamentally that have helped me so much as a manager that I've taken from motherhood. Um first of all try explaining anything to a toddler. >> Like if you're not very clear and you have to be succinct too, right? Like you're to if when you try and like explain gravity to a toddler, you've lost your audience. They're just gone, right? But if you're but if you do little teeny tiny bites and you're succinct and you're like this is what it is, then they can process. And I found that so helpful as a manager when you're taking a concept that your team doesn't understand or something that's complex and figuring out how to break it down into smaller pieces. And I think that that's why I've actually been really successful on TikTok as well because we take these complex ideas and we make them small. So, so anybody can digest it. So, anybody can take it and go, "Okay, that makes sense to me." Right? So, that's one thing I've learned. Um, I've learned also like priorities because you have got to figure out, you are going to as a mom, you're going to have 80 different priorities and everything's a fire. Everything's got to be done right now and you've got to figure out how to prioritize those things. I mean, you know, you're a mom. You've got to be like, how how am I going to be like eight places at once? >> Once. >> Yeah. >> Yes. >> And so, like leaning into understanding how to prioritize and how you let some things just drop like it's going to be okay. Right. >> Right. >> And then I I have a teen who is very into loopholes. like the kid could find a loophole anywhere and specificity has been like my and I think he's been like that since he's three. He just >> Yes. >> He's looked for those loopholes and um I remember one time we were we went out to dinner on the evening of Halloween and we went to dinner and the the server came and said, "Well, can we get you dessert?" And my son's like, "Yes." And I said, "No, honey. We're going trick-or-treating. you're going to have candy in like 20 minutes. And he's like, "But I don't have candy now." And I was like, "Okay, [Laughter] fair point, my boy." Fair point. >> Yes. Yes. Still a no, though. Still a no. >> Still a hard pass. But figuring out how to like guide through those loopholes because you are going to have employees who are going to be like, "But you weren't specific. You didn't say." So, learning specificity. Um, but I think the biggest lesson and I think this is the one that so many managers and parents probably need to always double down on is accountability, right? So when we >> when we have to hold our kids accountable, you know, and I remember being a kid and thinking like you're just doing this cuz you hate me and it's like no, I'm doing this because I love you, right? And >> yes, >> often it sucks for me too. holding you accountable sucks. >> Yes, totally. But it's so important and it's like I Oh, it's >> I learned that from my family before I learned that in business and now I brought that into business and it is fabulous. I have to say. >> Yeah. Because if you're not holding your people accountable, you cannot be you you can be successful for a while, but you cannot have sustained success if you do not understand accountability, not only for yourself, but for your people. And I think that that has been and and it sucks too. I mean, I'm sure you've been in a situation where you have to terminate somebody or you have to >> do like really hold them accountable and it's I mean it sucks for everybody. It's a terrible awful thing. >> It's always such a hard thing to do, but then you have to think of like the whole, you know, the whole organism, you know, is this best for the whole family? Is this best for the whole organization? And it's like, yeah, this is what's best for the whole. And that's like our job as the manager is to see oversee the whole, not just the individual. >> Exactly. >> But the whole big picture, right? >> Yeah. >> Yeah. Accountability is a big one. >> It It's a big mom one. >> It is. And I think it's also, you know, it's something you have to It's not something that you just learn and forget, right? Like it's something that you learn and you're constantly modifying and you're constantly applying. And you know what? And you're learning. It's it's just something that takes on a life of its own. I think >> Oh, yeah. Once the light bulb goes on with accountability, it's you'll never stop holding people accountable. I feel like that's once you fe once you figure out that this is a thing that is a real change maker in your home and in your in your work. Um once you tap into it, I was always just like avoid avoid. Oh my god, it's so stressful. I don't like confrontation. And um I think I could have been so much even so much more successful had I discovered accountability at a much younger age. >> I I >> because it's one of those things. >> I agree. And I think that it's just it's so crucial, but it also I think it creates more safety. And and that's the thing and I think that so many people are afraid of accountability because they're like, "Oh, I don't want to be like the dictator. I don't want to be this person." I'm like, I think it actually creates more safety with your team because your team knows that if they make a mistake that you will hold them accountable, but you will also hold others accountable and you'll hold yourself accountable. And I think that this this idea of constantly being afraid you're going to get in trouble at work. I think if you have a good culture of accountability, people aren't fearful. They know they know that like if I mess up, we're going to have a conversation about it. But they are less fearful than people who avoid those conflicts and avoid those conversations. >> Yeah. It's about consistent accountability. If if you can create consistency in accountability and it applies to all people, including yourself, then you're good to go. You know, you're not being a hypocrite. It's, you know, there's there has to be that consistent application of it, though. >> Yes, >> it does create the trust. You know, it's just like toddlers. It's one of the things kids, you know, they teach you how how they need boundaries, rules, you know, limitations. Give them boundaries and they feel safer. And it's the same with employees. They want to know what their code of conduct is. They want to know how to play within the parameter, you know, and it's that accountability is one of those major reinforcing things that just says, "Okay, you stepped over the boundary. Let's get back in it." >> Absolutely. Absolutely. >> Yeah. I love Yeah, that's amazing. So, we talked about also inspiration versus aspiration, which I thought this was interesting. We got into this. Could you make a distinction between inspiration and aspiration? And how do those two concepts show up differently in leadership? >> So inspiration is kind of that's your spark, right? That's the idea. That's the exciting time, right? And that's where you start to build with your teams, right? You've got this inspiration and you're going and everybody's excited. Aspiration, I think, is the hard part. Aspiration is where the rubber meets the road and that's your road map, right? So, you know, I think I think of like Simon Simick's book about, you know, the wise, the wise and the hows. I think it's kind of a why and a how, honestly. Like your inspiration is like this is what we're going to do and this is how we're going to go forward. But if you don't understand the aspiration, if you can't like really kind of continue to go through it and push that that agenda, then your inspiration really doesn't mean anything, right? So, and and I think that if you have to pick one between between each e either one of them, inspiration is wonderful, but I would pick your aspiration every single time because those are the, you know, when you think about your organization, those are the people who are really moving the needle. Those are the people who are really um showing up every day and doing the work and and doing the things. But if you have somebody who's got both pieces who can inspire you and get you to like do the things, those are the leaders that we follow, right? Those are the leaders we remember. Those and and those are the organizations like when you think from a branding perspective or people who are really kind of change makers, right? Those are the people, right? And so that's why having inspiration now and aspiration if you can pair them is like the golden ticket. >> Got it. So as a manager pairing both ideas and push pushing both of those ideas into our employees that are underneath us. >> Absolutely. >> See if we can Yeah. Give inspire them and aspire. >> Yes. >> I love that. So tactical missions. You talk about identifying tactical missions. What does that mean and how can a manager apply it in everyday work? >> So that's the meat and potatoes of what I teach, right? So I talk about the kind of how the long-term goal, right? Like when we think about who we want to be at our retirement party and what do we want people to say about us, you know, that again is that kind of inspiration where you're like, this is what I want people to think about and this is what I want them to do. That's all fine and good, but what happens when you have a poor performer and you have no idea what to do because you've never faced a poor performer or you have to you're faced with questions about personal matters, right? And you're just I watch so many managers get so caught up in their head, right, about oh, what do I do? And so what I really try and teach and I spend so much time trying to help people is and again I'm I'm using that parent metaphor again like you don't know what you don't know until you don't know, right? Like you've heard when you when you get your baby in the hospital they say don't shake your baby, right? And you're like I would never shake my baby. Why would I shake my baby? And they're like, they make you watch the purple video, the purple cry video before you leave the hospital, and you have to sign the paper. I don't know if they still do this. It's been a few years since I've had my babies, but you have to sign this video of like, don't shake your baby, right? And this is what's going to happen, and it's totally normal. And when you see this, don't freak out. Okay? Well, nobody does that with managers. Nobody does that. So, so as a parent, you get home and you're like cuddling your baby and you're so tired and you're doing the things and they're starting to cry and then you're like, I need to put the baby down. Right? Like it's crept into your mind because you spent this time with this tactical training of do not cross this line. Do not do this thing. Right? And it comes into your mind even though you were like why would I ever need this? Like this is my baby's so perfect. It's never going to happen. And then you're like, "Oh my god, I'm so glad I had this information." So I think of management the same way. You are going along and you're guiding humans, right? You're reacting to humans. You're interacting and suddenly something happens that you were like, "Where did this come from? How do I even deal with this?" And what we do because we're humans, we react. We just react. and we react in a way more often than not very poorly in like such a way that really digs us in a really bad spot. Um, but if you have talked about these scenarios, if you've talked about major hurdles that every manager will have, it doesn't mean it's going to be exactly specific to your scenario. It doesn't mean it's going to play out that same way. But what's going to happen when you talk about these kind of tactical missions and you talk about these tactical skills? They're going to sit back in your brain, right? They're sitting back in your brain. And when it comes up at work, instead of you shaking that employee, you can be like, "Okay, wait a minute. This is familiar. I'm not supposed to shake my employee, right? I remember I learned about this thing or I heard about this thing." and it doesn't need to be the dramatic scenario that I'm building up in my mind. Right? So, if we could spend more time on those tactical pieces and really like we can talk about the work of a manager. The work of a manager is you support your team, you make strategic decisions, and you go home. But that's not the work, right? That's not the work. And I think that spending time understanding like the tactical things, the actual nittygritty of the job, if we could spend more time understanding that, and you can't get that from all the books, right? Like you can't get that. And that's what I try and bring. I try and bring 20 years of my experience of going like, "Hey, I've seen this scenario time and time again. Let's prepare you for it so you don't shake that human when you have that scenario. >> I love that. I love that. I think that works in so many different aspects of our lives. >> And and in general, don't shake humans. Just like the overall >> don't shake your humans. >> Also, just showing up prepared. I had another guest who actually worked with men. It was my only male guest I've had so far on the manager. And that's he actually teaches husbands how to show up prepared, mentally prepared for difficult conversations that like he's like you got to have that na Navy Seal mindset.
